disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Chronic shallow ankle (medial) ulcer with brown staining and hardened lower-leg skin, pain RELIEVED by elevation
trigger_question_examples: Is the ulcer over the inner ankle with brown staining/hardened skin, and is the pain relieved by putting the leg up?
Does elevating the leg help the pain?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, it's over the inner ankle with brown staining and hardened skin, and the pain eases when I put the leg up.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: A NEW rim of tender, warm, spreading redness around the ulcer with increased smelly exudate and throbbing pain (secondary skin infection)
trigger_question_examples: Is there a new area of tender, warm, spreading redness around the ulcer, with more smelly discharge and throbbing pain?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, a new rim of tender warm redness around the ulcer, much more smelly discharge, and new throbbing pain.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No rapidly ascending calf redness, no high fever/rigors, no leg-wide severe swelling (sepsis/deep-infection screen)
trigger_question_examples: Is the redness rapidly climbing up the calf toward the knee, any high fever/shakes, or the whole leg swollen and severely painful?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, the redness isn't racing up the calf, no high fever or shakes, and the whole leg isn't hugely swollen.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Should I put my tight compression stockings back on to help it heal?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Advise elevation now, but do NOT resume tight compression during the acute infection/severe pain — compression is resumed once the acute cellulitis settles and only after arterial disease is excluded (ABPI). Explain elevation is correct for a venous (not arterial) ulcer.
